question: Explain the process of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST). Use a small example to illustrate the steps, including how you handle the root node and traverse the tree.
expectedPoints: Start at the root node and compare the new value with the current node.
If the new value is less, go to the left child; if greater, go to the right child.
Repeat the process until an empty spot (null pointer) is found, then insert the new node there.
Example: Inserting value '5' into a BST with root 10 (go left), then left child 7 (go left again), then insert as left child of 7.

question: Compare and contrast the time complexity of the search operation in a balanced Binary Search Tree (e.g., an AVL tree) versus a hash table that uses chaining for collision resolution. Discuss both the average and worst-case scenarios.
expectedPoints: Balanced BST: Search is O(log n) in both average and worst-case due to maintaining balance and height.
Hash Table (with chaining): Search is O(1) on average, assuming a good hash function and load factor.
Hash Table worst-case: O(n) if all keys hash to the same bucket, degrading to a linked list.
Conclusion: Hash tables offer better average performance, while balanced BSTs provide more reliable worst-case performance.

question: Define a stack data structure and name two of its key operations. Provide one real-world application where a stack would be an ideal choice and explain why.
expectedPoints: Definition: A LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) linear data structure.
Key operations: push (add an element) and pop (remove the top element).
Application: The 'Undo' feature in software (e.g., a text editor).
Explanation: Each action is pushed onto the stack. The most recent action (last in) is the first to be undone (first out) when the user triggers undo.
